bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., 'Anecdota Literaria: Proverbs from Ms. Harley 3362, of the end of the fifteenth century,' The Retrospective Review, new series 2, no. VII (May 1854): 309.
bibliographic.title: Anecdota Literaria: Proverbs from Ms. Harley 3362, of the end of the fifteenth century

entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00128: a one-page item in the Anecdota Literaria sequence, headed PROVERBS, introduced as from Harley MS 3362 of the end of the fifteenth century, and signed W. DIMEV's bibliography attributes the item to Thomas Wright as editor.
entities.label: Thomas Wright, Proverbs from MS. Harley 3362 (May 1854)
notes: The printed page itself signs the item only W.; Thomas Wright is the DIMEV bibliographic attribution and is not a claim of medieval authorship.
The item begins below the preceding contribution on printed p.309 and ends at the page foot; the next item begins on p.310.

rights.basis: The item was published in London in 1854 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.

entities.description: A comparative study and edition of the bilingual proverbs in Cambridge, Trinity College MS O.2.45. Its notes quote several Harley MS 3362 parallels relevant to CME00128 from John Mitchell Kemble's earlier transcription, using an old foliation system. It is supporting scholarship, not an immediate source of the U-M encoding or Wright's 1854 item.
entities.label: Max Förster, Frühmittelenglische Sprichwörter (1902)
notes: The Harley parallels cite Kemble and use old folio numbers 3, 5, 6, and 7 for target readings; those numbers are not silently converted into current British Library/DIMEV foliation.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1902 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: The 1854 source calls the manuscript end of the fifteenth century; the current catalog supplies only the broader century.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century English collection of macaronic poems, songs, proverbs, riddles, grammatical rules, and courtly lyrics. The opening proverb run begins on f.2r. DIMEV securely places target readings on f.2v and f.4v and associates further target text with ff.4–5, but the exact continuous start and end surfaces of Wright's 31-saying selection remain unresolved without manuscript images or a direct folio-by-folio concordance.

holding.notes: All manuscripts and archives are consulted at the British Library in London; current physical requests require a valid Reader Pass and the exact shelfmark.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 3362

notes: The current catalog identifies Gothic script and English origin.
The present binding is a post-1600 British Museum in-house binding.
The catalog's broad ff.2r–79v contents range is not the target selection's extent.

entities.description: Versioned scholarly index records mapping several sayings on the printed page to separately circulating items: secure exact matches in records 6052, 109.5, and 6212; prose or prosified associations in records 5532 and 6523; and an overlapping incomplete fragment in record 5230. The release indexes the entire codex more broadly and does not identify the 31-saying selection as one work.
entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 target-relevant records for Harley MS 3362
notes: DIMEV's manuscript index has 16 witness entries for Harley MS 3362 across the codex; those entries are not a count of sayings on the target page.
The legacy or commented loci for the prosified field and dog sayings conflict with current ghost-note loci; both conflicts are preserved as open questions.

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a CC BY 4.0 license.

next_steps: Inspect complete color images of the opening proverb run and build a line-by-line concordance from the printed incipit through explicit.
Use f.2v and f.4v only for readings DIMEV securely locates there; retain ff.2r–5 as a qualified search range rather than a confirmed continuous extent.
Do not infer that all 33 XML L elements occupy one folio or that the catalog's broad ff.2r–79v contents range is target extent.
openQuestions.question: Which exact recto, verso, and column surfaces contain all 31 sayings in Wright's sequence, and did Wright preserve a continuous manuscript run?

next_steps: Request the complete current object: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, every recto and verso of fols.1–91, edges where offered, and detached matter.
Map the work separately to the opening target run and retain the supplied image inventory, quotation, correspondence, and exact license.
Do not describe an order as pending or permissions as granted until an actual request receives a response.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply or authorize a complete color digital facsimile of the entire current physical Harley MS 3362, and what exact storage, Git-publication, viewer, print-on-demand, and commercial permissions would govern it?

next_steps: Compare each elliptical item directly with manuscript images and later editions or transcriptions.
Keep DIMEV's explicit incomplete label for 'Wel wot the cat' separate from the five ellipses whose cause is not established.
Do not normalize the dots into manuscript damage, illegibility, or deliberate excision without source evidence.
openQuestions.question: What does each of the six printed ellipses represent in the manuscript and Wright's editorial process?

next_steps: Preserve record-5532 and record-6523 as prose/prosified associations rather than active secure verse witnesses.
Preserve record-5230 as an overlapping incomplete fragment of the final saying rather than silently choosing it over record-6523.
Check manuscript images before choosing between f.4 and f.5 for the field saying or f.5 and legacy f.7 for the dog saying.
Do not map the target foot/shoe line to record-6520: DIMEV explicitly says 4079.3 is not in this manuscript.
openQuestions.question: How should the target's prosified sayings, overlapping fragments, and conflicting DIMEV legacy loci be reconciled in a future item-level model?

next_steps: Keep the current British Library date and English origin at the witness layer.
Treat Ely as a tentative scribal dialect region, not a place of composition or compilation.
Consult manuscript images and current linguistic scholarship before assigning a narrower work origin or date.
openQuestions.question: When and where was this exact proverb sequence compiled, and does DIMEV's manuscript-level Ely dialect localization apply uniformly to the target run?

relations.subjectLabel: DIMEV v1.0 target-relevant records for Harley MS 3362

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00128.xml. The U-M encoding, Wright's serial item, the bound volume, the Internet Archive scan, Harley MS 3362, and the scholarly catalog records are separate entities.

The cataloged historical unit is the exact 31-saying selection printed in 1854, not all proverbial material in Harley MS 3362 and not one ideal DIMEV work. The 33 XML L elements represent 31 displayed sayings because print lines 8 and 10 are indented continuations of lines 7 and 9.
DIMEV treats some exact Harley readings as verse couplets and others as prose or prosified associations. XML LG/L serialization therefore does not establish that every saying is verse.
The six printed ellipses are preserved, but the available edition and catalog do not establish whether each marks illegibility, physical loss, editorial omission, or an incomplete exemplar.

The current British Library record marks Digitised Content as No and exposes no viewer or IIIF route as of 2026-07-13. This dated negative finding does not prove that no unpublished, internal, or legacy photography exists.
The pinned CME root LICENSE uses Unlicense language framed as software. Its application to corpus XML/data/markup remains unresolved and is not broadened into a high-confidence rights claim.

sourceLinks.provider: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a CC BY 4.0 license.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation and version of University of Michigan CME00128, which encodes the one-page PROVERBS item printed on p.309 of The Retrospective Review, new series no. VII (May 1854). DIMEV attributes the signed W. item to editor Thomas Wright. The print selects 31 Middle English sayings from British Library Harley MS 3362 and displays them in 33 lines, two of which are indented continuations. The exact continuous manuscript locus is not established by the edition or current public catalog: target-relevant DIMEV records securely identify readings on ff.2v and 4v, while prosified or fragment associations involve ff.4–5 and expose conflicting legacy loci. Complete supporting scans of the 1854 serial carrier and Förster's 1902 comparative article, plus DIMEV v1.0, are cached with fixity. The current British Library record exposes no public digital images; onsite consultation and paid whole-object photography remain request routes.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Compilation no later than the fifteenth-century witness; individual sayings may be older; first known publication of this selection May 1854
catalogSummary.editor: Thomas Wright (attributed by DIMEV from the printed signature W.)
catalogSummary.region: England; exact compilation place unknown; Harley MS 3362 scribal language localized by DIMEV/LALME to Ely
catalogSummary.title: Proverbs from Harley MS 3362 (Wright's 1854 selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Proverbs from Harley MS 3362 (Wright's 1854 selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact sequence of 31 Middle English proverbial sayings printed from British Library Harley MS 3362 on p.309 of The Retrospective Review in May 1854 and encoded as CME00128. It is modeled as an anonymous manuscript-specific compilation or editorial selection of distinct sayings, not as one stable authorial poem, not as all proverbial material in Harley MS 3362, and not as one DIMEV work. The manuscript witness, Wright's edition, bound serial volume, scan, U-M encoding, and repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Thirty-one-saying proverb sequence

locators.value: p.309, from the centered PROVERBS heading below the preceding item's rule through signed W. and the rule at the page foot
PDF page 759 / Internet Archive leaf n758
opening proverb run, provisionally ff.2r–5; secure target readings at f.2v and f.4v; exact continuous boundaries unresolved
DIV1/LG[1], 33 L display units representing 31 visually delimited sayings

notes: Complete means complete relative to Wright's printed selection. The exact manuscript surface inventory and the original compilation boundary remain unresolved.
The six printed ellipses are preserved without assigning a cause.
contentParts.summary: The complete manuscript-derived sequence printed under the heading PROVERBS, from 'Ȝoung seynt, old devyl' through 'Whyl the dogge gnawyth, the cat wolde ete.'

assertion.notes: The witness supplies a terminus ante quem for the represented sequence, not an origin date for every proverb. Wright's end-of-century wording is narrower than the current British Library's whole-century date.

dateStatements.display: No later than the fifteenth-century witness (1400–1499); exact compilation date unknown

notes: This confirms that the sayings represented by the cataloged selection survive in a fifteenth-century witness. It does not confirm that Wright's exact 31-item selection already existed as an autonomous manuscript unit. Individual sayings have separate transmission histories and may be older.

scope.description: The represented sayings' survival in the witness, not a confirmed autonomous boundary for Wright's exact 31-item selection

assertion.notes: This does not claim first publication of every independently circulating proverb.

dateStatements.display: May 1854 (first known publication of this exact selection)

extent.description: The complete selection has 31 visually delimited sayings in one unnumbered section. Print and XML contain 33 display-line units because two sayings have indented continuation lines. The XML has one LG and 33 L elements, but these are representation metrics rather than a secure claim of 33 verse lines; DIMEV classifies only a subset as verse and identifies other exact Harley readings as prose or prosified.

formStatements.description: The selection combines separately indexed proverbial couplets with one-line sayings and readings that DIMEV classifies as prose or prosified. The 33 XML L elements in one LG reproduce print display units but do not establish that every item is verse.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in the heading and headnote belongs to the 1854 editorial layer; the cataloged sayings are Middle English.
The preferred title is supplied for disambiguation; the generic printed heading is retained separately as PROVERBS.
No single DIMEV or IMEV/NIMEV identifier is assigned to the 31-saying whole. Related-work identifiers apply only to specific sayings or materially overlapping fragments.
The manuscript witness date, English origin, and Ely scribal-language localization are not silently transferred to every proverb's composition.

The source page is shared: the target occupies the middle and lower portion of printed p.309, not the whole physical page. Complete source coverage means the entire one-page Wright item from heading through final rule.
A complete manuscript facsimile should cover the whole current physical object and map the work's opening-folio surfaces separately.

next_steps: Inspect complete color images of the opening proverb run and make a line-by-line concordance.
Keep secure DIMEV f.2v and f.4v locators distinct from qualified ff.4–5 prose/prosified associations.
Reconcile current BL/DIMEV foliation with Förster's Kemble-derived old fols.3, 5, 6, and 7 before assigning a continuous target range.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript surfaces and sequence boundary correspond to Wright's 31 sayings?

next_steps: Compare the exact manuscript order with the printed page once images are available.
Retain compilation as the cataloging unit while qualifying the preferred title with Wright's 1854 selection.
Do not infer continuity solely from the compact print layout or XML LG.
openQuestions.question: How much of the ordering and boundary belongs to the medieval manuscript compilation, and how much to Wright's editorial selection?

next_steps: Add item-level identities only where boundaries and correspondence are secure and useful for discovery.
Preserve the record-6523 prosified full-line association and record-5230 incomplete-fragment overlap together.
Do not map the target shoe/foot saying to DIMEV record 6520 because DIMEV explicitly rejects a Harley witness for 4079.3.
openQuestions.question: Which remaining sayings have secure independent index identities, and how should the prosified and overlapping final items be modeled?

next_steps: Compare each elliptical saying with manuscript images and later transcriptions.

openQuestions.question: When and where was this exact sequence compiled, and does the manuscript-level Ely localization apply uniformly to the target run?

assertion.notes: The witness is English and the sayings are Middle English; the precise compilation place remains unknown.
DIMEV reports LALME/eLALME localization 1.112 Ely for the manuscript. This is a scribal-language association and is not transferred to authorial origin or place of compilation.
Anonymous is a display responsibility, not a claim that one unidentified person authored all 31 sayings.

scope.description: The constituent sayings, which may have multiple independent origins

assertion.notes: Compiler is the most useful role for a manuscript-specific sequence of independently circulating sayings, although the precise boundary between manuscript compilation and Wright's editorial selection remains unresolved.
The print signs the item W.; DIMEV expands this through bibliography entry Wright1854b.
The exact editorial workflow and use of any intermediary transcript are not stated on the page.

scope.description: The represented readings selected from Harley MS 3362

subjects.label: Conduct of life and practical wisdom
Fortune, divine disposition, necessity, and appetite
Social observation, envy, self-interest, and unequal reward

summaries.value: Thirty-one compact sayings offer practical and social observations about youthful sanctity and later vice, silence and gossip, hardship, experience, memory, prudence, envy, necessity, appetite, self-interest, unequal reward, and divine disposition. Familiar images include the tongue that breaks bone, the absent cat, the burned person's fear of fire, God disposing what humans propose, the field's eye and wood's ear, the bird in hand, the cat that knows whose beard it licks, and the dog and cat competing for food.

The 1854 page contains 31 visually delimited sayings: 29 one-line items and two two-line units, 'Tunge brekyth bon / thow hyrself have non' and 'Labbe hyt whyste / and owt yt muste.' CME00128 serializes the 33 display lines as L elements in one LG. Six sayings end in ellipses; only the cat-beard item is explicitly labeled incomplete by DIMEV.

The record preserves the exact 31-saying printed sequence as one compilation while separately linking only secure or materially important DIMEV identities. It does not explode all 33 XML lines into independent works, treat the 16 DIMEV Harley witness-index entries as target items, or force a single verse classification onto prose and prosified readings.

assertion.notes: The witness and editor qualifier distinguishes this exact 31-saying selection from other proverb compilations; it is not a manuscript title.

notes: This is the generic printed heading, not evidence for an original manuscript rubric.

titles.value: Whyl the dogge gnawyth, the cat wolde ete
